evidence: Dependency direction is clean: api imports from exploration/query/config/model; exploration imports from query/config/model; query imports from model; config imports from model. No reverse imports found in any direction.
No import cycles: the dependency graph is a strict DAG.
SemanticFieldExplorer is public; MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private, correctly hiding internal collaborators from the api layer.

api.SketchHandlers calls RelationPatternBuilder.buildFullPattern() at lines 206 and 239, then calls executor.executeSurfacePattern() directly — the api handler both constructs domain objects (BCQL patterns) and executes queries, acting as a domain service for the sketch path.
api.ConcordanceHandlers also calls RelationPatternBuilder.buildFullPattern() (line 59), bypassing any domain-layer intermediary for concordance queries.

By contrast, the exploration path (ExplorationHandlers) correctly delegates all domain logic to SemanticFieldExplorer — creating an inconsistency in how the two halves of the api layer are structured.

RadialPlot.java lives in api/ (src/main/java/pl/marcinmilkowski/word_sketch/api/RadialPlot.java) rather than a viz/ or utils/ package. It is an SVG rendering utility with no HTTP concerns, so its placement in api blurs that package boundary.

WordSketchApiServer convenience constructor instantiates SemanticFieldExplorer directly — the api layer knows the concrete exploration-layer class by name. The full constructor allows injection, but production startup always uses the convenience form.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: The sketch and concordance paths in the api layer directly build BCQL patterns and execute queries themselves rather than delegating to a domain service, creating a structural asymmetry with the exploration path. This means changes to pattern-building logic or query execution have two contact points in the api layer rather than one domain-layer owner.

strengths: Dependency direction is enforced consistently: api → exploration → query → model, with no upward references anywhere in the codebase.
SemanticFieldExplorer correctly hides MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator as package-private, giving the api layer a single stable entry point for the exploration subsystem.
QueryExecutor is an interface that acts as a clean architectural boundary between the api/exploration layers and the BlackLab implementation, enabling test isolation.
The model package is genuinely clean: no persistence, HTTP, or I/O concerns appear in it, and this is enforced by all callers importing model types only as data.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The issues are localized asymmetries rather than systemic violations: the sketch and concordance paths in the api layer act as domain services (building patterns, executing queries) while the exploration path delegates to a proper domain service, creating an inconsistency of design pattern within the same package; and RadialPlot is misplaced in api rather than a utility package.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has a well-understood layered architecture with no cycles and no upward references — characteristics of a solid (80-range) design. What pulls it below 80 is the consistent asymmetry in how the api layer handles its two main query paths: exploration queries flow through a proper domain facade, but sketch and concordance queries have the api handler doing pattern construction and direct query execution, mixing HTTP and domain concerns in a repeated pattern across SketchHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers. This is a real structural issue but not a deep blast-radius problem since it is contained within two handler files. The RadialPlot misplacement is a minor boundary blurring. Taken together these put the codebase at 72 — better than 60 (which would indicate significant drag) but short of 80 (which would require more consistent boundary discipline).

issues.summary: SketchHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers do domain work (pattern building + query execution) that should live in a domain service
evidence: SketchHandlers.java lines 206-207: calls RelationPatternBuilder.buildFullPattern(rel, lemma) then executor.executeSurfacePattern(...) directly inside the HTTP handler method.
SketchHandlers.java lines 239-240: same pattern repeated for the full-sketch case.
ConcordanceHandlers.java line 59: calls RelationPatternBuilder.buildFullPattern(rel.get(), req.seed()) directly in the handler.
By contrast, ExplorationHandlers delegates all domain logic to SemanticFieldExplorer, keeping the handler free of domain object construction.
issues.suggestion: Extract a SketchQueryService (or WordSketchService) in the exploration or query package that owns the pattern-build + execute-surface-pattern coordination for sketch and concordance queries. SketchHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers would then delegate to this service in the same way ExplorationHandlers delegates to SemanticFieldExplorer, eliminating the asymmetry and giving pattern-building logic a single domain-layer owner.

issues.summary: RadialPlot SVG rendering utility is placed in the api package instead of a dedicated rendering or utils package
evidence: RadialPlot.java lives at api/RadialPlot.java and contains SVG string-building logic with no HTTP-specific concerns.
The CLAUDE.md documentation records the intended location as viz/RadialPlot.java, confirming the placement is not deliberate.
The api package's stated role (HTTP handlers, routing, response assembly) does not include rendering utilities.
issues.suggestion: Move RadialPlot to a new viz/ package (matching the documented intent in CLAUDE.md) or to utils/ if a full viz package is not warranted. Update VisualizationHandlers to import it from the new location.

root_causes: The exploration feature was built with a proper domain facade (SemanticFieldExplorer) from the start, but the earlier sketch/concordance query paths were implemented directly in handlers before the facade pattern was established, leaving them unrestructured.
Package boundaries are expressed as directory conventions rather than enforced by module-level visibility (e.g., Java modules), so placing RadialPlot in api has no compile-time consequence.
